You can think of XHB as a "picks and shovels" fund. There is a large allocation in Building Products, almost two times the one of ITB, and I think this is relevant because it provides you with a way of hedging the volatility of new housing starts. Companies in the Building Products segment are providing crucial components (HVAC, insulation, roofing, etc…), making it still indirectly exposed to new housing starts but also to the maintenance industry, which is highly relevant as the housing stock in the US is aging. I believe that we are going to see a "Renovation Renaissance", with a surge of repairs and remodeling, and XHB is perfectly positioned to benefit from it.

In addition, XHB is more exposed to the Home furnishing Retail segment, which is a high beta segment that could prove to be a source of upside while ITB also has a retail exposure but through home improvement warehouses. XHB gets you more exposure to discretionary spending, which is very relevant in a soft landing scenario with growth attached to consumer confidence and wages against pure exposure to housing starts like ITB.

As mentioned, ITB can be seen as a Homebuilding focused fund. This is not necessarily a bad positioning, and it has been very relevant in an environment with housing inventory being squeezed. However, now that cost is an increasing consideration, margins are under pressure and ITB will only outperform if the velocity of new building contracts is sustained. This might happen, but this is not a bet that I am really going to take. There is something unique about ITB’s exposure to Specialty Chemicals (a fancy wording for paint companies) and Construction Materials, but these segments are too niche and underrepresented to really make a difference against the exposure to Homebuilding.

Pricing The Recovery

As XHB and ITB are close competitors, they are aligned in terms of pricing. However, I do think that it is surprising to see XHB showing a 0.35% expense ratio, lower than ITB’s 0.38% despite the fact that XHB’s AUM is $1.7 billion, smaller than ITB’s $2.6 billion. In terms of liquidity, the two funds are really close, with a median bid/ask spread of 0.04% for XHB and 0.03% for ITB. In both cases, if you are worried about liquidity, XHB and ITB are good options.

Looking at valuations, ITB is trading with a P/E ratio of 14.87x and a P/B ratio of 1.97x against 1.06x and 2.00x for XHB. I do see the fact that the P/B ratios are aligned but that the P/E ratio of ITB is lower as a sign that the market is pricing the now lower enthusiasm about homebuilders, and highlighting the relevance of a diversified approach. In addition, I don’t necessarily think that XHB is more expensive because the industrial and retail sectors of the fund are bringing quality and resilient earnings, which is naturally something that has a price. The companies that are selling products are priced at higher points than homebuilders because they are holding intellectual property moats, while the homebuilder activity can be “easily” replicated (i.e. lower barriers to entry).

Finally, the dividends are giving a small edge to XHB with a yield of 0.76%, 0.13 percentage points above ITB’s 0.63%. This is a secondary consideration to the performance, mainly capital gains, that we are going to address below.

Historical Performance And Risk Analysis

Let’s start by taking a look at total returns.

As you can see, XHB has been an outperformer in the short to medium term (all time frames below 5Y), while ITB has been more relevant over the very long term (all time frames above 10Y). From this, we can say that XHB’s diversified strategy is working better in times of volatility and uncertainty, while ITB is relevant when the homebuilding market is strong. If we return to an environment with inventories under pressure, strong housing starts and low pressure on margins, ITB will be the best fund again, but for now, the positioning of XHB wins.

Even in the long term, XHB has always provided investors with a lower volatility than ITB, even if it remains at a high point. We measure it with standard deviations: 27.90% (3 years), 27.48% (5 years), and 26.50% (10 years) for XHB against 30.12% (3 years), 29.64% (5 years), and 29.01% (10 years) for ITB. The consequence of the higher returns and lower volatility metrics of XHB are stronger risk-adjusted returns on the 3Y and 5Y time frames, and 10Y metrics actually show us that the outperformance of ITB is not as impressive as it seems. We measure this with Sharpe ratios: 0.66 (3 years), 0.51 (5 years), and 0.50 (10 years) for XHB against 0.61 (3 years), 0.48 (5 years), and 0.54 (10 years) for ITB.

You can argue that the difference in Sharpe ratios is not very impressive, but sharpe ratios are not taking capital preservation into account. Here, XHB is ahead again, with a maximum drawdown of -35.89% over the last five years and -36.35 over the last ten years against -36.82% and -38.92% for ITB.

The Case For A Broad Recovery

I believe that the macroeconomic environment is now establishing a case for breadth against concentration, as the housing deficit is still the norm, but the implications of this deficit are shifting.

As a wide population of Americans is now locked with mortgages below 4%, the housing mobility is reaching historic lows. This is a fact favoring the Building Products and Home Improvement sectors that you will find more heavily represented in XHB’s portfolio, as the impossibility to move is fueling the will to improve (increase size, upgrade amenities, or replace roofs). For companies such as Masco Corp and Owens Corning, this is an especially dynamic environment with thriving remodeling growth.

We saw during the pandemic that supply chain exposed companies were a source of pain, but the current dynamic is favoring margin expansion for such companies, notably for industrial sub-sectors that are benefiting from a stabilization of input costs but a still important pricing power. This implies that there is a potential for further improvements of margins, even with limited revenue growth. At the other end, homebuilders at the end of the supply chain are not benefiting from the current environment, as for them, it means increased land costs and shortages in the labor market. This is why I like the broad reach of XHB better.

Finally, the high-interest rate environment could be sustained for longer than expected, as hinted by the fact that long term interest rates are not going down despite recent cuts. A return to the pre-COVID zero interest rate environment is not going to happen anytime soon, and this limits the pricing power of homebuilders. ITB’s holdings are less flexible in such an environment, while the retail and furnishing exposure of XHB can leverage changes in consumer employment and purchasing power, moving faster than mortgage rates.

Risks

The primary risk that could affect both XHB and ITB is a potential increase in inflation following lowered short-term interest rates, as the inflation is proving to be stickier than expected. The consequence could be an increased mortgage rate that would interrupt the new construction and remodeling activity.

More specific to XHB, an appeal of the fund is that the fund is exposed to retail themes, and it allows for a better diversification against risks driven by mortgage rates, but this also introduces new risks if discretionary spending were to decrease. A recessionary environment in the US or increased unemployment metrics would hurt furniture sales more than new home contracts, making XHB a worse investment than ITB. In the soft landing scenario currently unfolding, XHB wins, but in a “hard” landing scenario, ITB would be hurt less.

Conclusion

My buy rating for the State Street® SPDR® S&P® Homebuilders ETF is rooted in the fact that the equal weight methodology is more effective at capturing the whole “house” value chain (building products and retail), making it a strong ETF to benefit from a potential "Renovation Renaissance" induced by Americans locked in sub 4% mortgage rates that could happen if the soft landing scenario happens. In front of that, I only give a hold rating to the iShares U.S. Home Construction ETF because it's positioning is weakened by still high-interest rates, and pressures on construction costs that are hurting homebuilders.

data.title: PayPal: A Re-Rating Is Inevitable (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

In early 2024, I initiated coverage on fintech giant - PayPal Holdings, Inc. (PYPL) - with a Buy rating in the mid-$60s:

Based on our fundamental, quantitative, technical, and valuation analysis, PayPal is a solid investment opportunity at ~12x forward P/E and not a value trap as many market pundits believe. Now, PayPal is no Meta [of late-2022], but investors buying in here could reasonably expect to generate 20%+ CAGR return in PYPL stock.

Key Takeaway: I rate PayPal a "Buy" in the $60s, with a strong preference for staggered accumulation via a 3-6 month DCA plan.

Source: The PayPal Dilemma: Value Trap Horror Or Investment Bliss?

Now, while this contrarian call worked like a charm in 2024 [PayPal stock surged over 40%+ from our initial entry point within 12 months], the bet has soured this year as PayPal stock has roundtripped all the way back into the low-$60s/high-$50s -- diverging from the broader equity market, i.e., S&P 500 (SPY), which has continued scaling new all-time highs in recent months:

In today's note, we reassess PayPal's latest business trends and valuation to formulate an informed investment decision.

PayPal: By The Numbers

Jeff Bezos [Amazon (AMZN) founder] has famously said -

The stock is not the company, and the company is not the stock.

And this quote fits PayPal like a hand to glove.

While its recent stock performance has been terrible, PayPal's business has never been larger or more profitable than it is right now!

For Q3 2025, PayPal reported a double beat -

  • Revenues: $8.42B [+7% y/y (acceleration), vs. est. $8.24B].
  • Normalized EPS: $1.34 per share [+12% y/y, vs. est. $1.21 per share].

Now, PayPal's platform usage frequency (measured by the number of transactions) was down -5% y/y [primarily due to management's pricing actions within PayPal's PSP (payment service provider) business]; however, PayPal's total payment volume grew +8% y/y to a record $458B and transaction margin dollars rose +6% y/y to $3.9B in Q3 2025 - powered by healthy growth across the platform - Branded Experiences, Venmo, PSP, and debit cards.

Consequently, PayPal generated $1.7B in free cash flow [$2.3B in adj. free cash flow], out of which it returned $1.5B to shareholders via stock buybacks.

Additionally, PayPal's management raised its FY25 outlook for transaction margin dollars to $15.45-$15.55B and non-GAAP EPS to $5.35-5.39 per share:

That said, PayPal's management did flag macro-induced weakness in consumer spending observed in early Q4 2025, which explains PayPal's softish +2-5% y/y volume growth outlook for the ongoing quarter.

Now, PayPal's near-term business outlook is mired in uncertainty; however, ongoing momentum within Venmo and BNPL, coupled with PayPal's next-gen growth vectors - PayPal World, Agentic Commerce (partnerships with OpenAI, Google, Mastercard, and Perplexity in place), Ads, Crypto, and banking (if approved) - can continue to drive mid-single digit growth at the fintech for years to come.

As per consensus estimates, PayPal's top-line growth rate is projected to tick up in 2026 and 2027, with earnings growth primed to exceed sales growth.

PYPL Stock Is Deeply Discounted

With its market capitalization sitting at ~$56B [$59.86 per share], PayPal is trading at a bargain ~8-9x 2025E FCF of $6-7B, i.e., FCF yield of ~11-12.5%.

And, on an earnings basis, PayPal is trading at ~11x forward EPS, versus the S&P-500, which is trading at ~23x heading into 2026.

Now, bears would quickly point to PayPal's modest, single-digit top-line growth rate as a justification for its lowly trading multiples and apparent relative valuation discount; however, PayPal's ~5% y/y growth rate for 2025 is higher than the S&P-500's median y/y growth rate.

Furthermore, PayPal looks like a bargain even on an absolute basis.

Using conservative assumptions for future growth [5-yr sales CAGR: 5%] and margins [optimized FCF margin: 20%], TQI's Valuation model pegs PayPal's fair value at $91.4 per share [$88B market cap], i.e., ~53% higher from current levels.

Now, predicting where a stock will trade in the short term is impossible; however, over the long run, a stock will track its business fundamentals and obey the immutable laws of money. If the interest rates were to return to artificially low levels (i.e., ZIRP), higher equity multiples would be justifiable. However, I work with the assumption that interest rates will eventually track the long-term average of ~5%. Inverting this number, we get a trading multiple of ~20x (P/FCF).

Assuming a discounted exit multiple of ~15x P/FCF [to bake in PayPal's paltry growth profile], I see PayPal's stock trading at ~$206 per share five years from now. This price target implies a five-year CAGR return of ~28% and handily exceeds our investment hurdle rate of 15%.

In light of PayPal's steep year-to-date decline, PYPL's 5-year expected CAGR return has improved to ~28%, rendering it a Strong Buy under TQI's valuation process.

Concluding Thoughts

PayPal's cocktail of mid-to-high single-digit top line growth, transaction & operating margin expansion, low starting valuation, and a humongous capital return program [~10% stock buyback yield [TTM], freshly initiated quarterly dividend] is a recipe for robust shareholder returns over the next several years. Consequently, I am upgrading PayPal stock to a Strong Buy here.

Key Takeaway: I rate PayPal a Strong Buy in the high-$50s/low-$60s.

Thank you for reading, and happy investing! Please share any questions, thoughts, and/or concerns in the comments section below.

data.title: Power Solutions International: Valuation Has Reset, The Thesis Is Improving
data.description:
Power Solutions International, Inc. (PSIX) has turned into a cautious Buy amidst a backdrop of a significant valuation reset (despite strong Q3 earnings and topline beat) and confirmation of growth signals, primarily led by its power systems and data center demand. When I rated PSIX a Hold in September, I was mostly wary of the valuations. I was also looking for sustained signals of growth and income. Growth and income trends did come up strongly in Q3 2025, but the gross margins trend is still an open concern.

Revisiting The Thesis

One of the core ideas around the Hold rating in September was the fact that I found merit in the narrative of PSIX’s pivot from industrial engines to data-center power systems. The issue was with market's front-running validation, treating early traction signs almost like durable demand. The pivot's legitimacy remains intact, but the story has not progressed to the next stage of proof of concept. Not that I was expecting material progress within a quarter, but the fact remains that the stage of the company (with respect to the data center specific pivot) has not moved materially. However, some of the trends appear more confirmed now.

Since the original thesis, PSIX has delivered a second consecutive quarter of strong revenue and earnings growth, with management explicitly citing robust data center demand and capacity expansion. This provides early evidence that the Q2 breakout was not a one-off event. However, while demand momentum is increasingly credible, visibility into backlog durability or contract cadence remains limited.

The second quarter of outsized revenue growth does alter the Hold view, with fundamental progress being made (although still not sustained detailed evidence of a step change in the company's operating environment due to data center demand). Revenues were up 62% YoY in Q3 FY2025, ticking the multi-quarter revenue growth criteria I had on my checklist (as stated in my previous thesis).

Interestingly, the revenue growth was achieved despite softness in industrial and transportation segments, signaling a potential shift to higher growth markets. The outlook shared for the full year emphasized continued growth in the power systems end market including products for data centers, even as the industrial and transportation demand stays flat.

The Company anticipates strong sales growth of 45% for 2025 compared to 2024, driven by expected growth in the power systems end market including products supporting data centers, while sales in the industrial and transportation end markets are projected to remain flat.

I was even waiting for more confirmation around a net income spike seen in Q2 that was driven by a deferred tax asset release. In Q3, a net income growth of 59% YoY now confirms that operating earnings are indeed improving consistently. The scale of tax related distortion is significantly lower in Q3 ($7m in Q3 versus $29.2m in Q2). The operating leverage trends seen in SG&A expenses have also progressed meaningfully. SG&A rose 39% YoY, well below revenue growth. SG&A as a % of sales declined to 7.5% from 8.7%, implying a meaningful, if not sustained, efficiency gain.

The only drag that has emerged is gross margins, which declined to 23.9% vs 28.9% last year. How much of this is due to a mix shift towards lower margin products remains to be seen because the topline growth has happened in power systems (and data center products). If this is the growth area and comes with lower margins, then the bar for growth in this segment increases to compensate for lower margins. However, if it is primarily due to "temporary inefficiencies from accelerated production ramp", a couple of quarters of watching margins will help ease concerns around pricing power and competitive pressures. In fact, PSIX's competitive positioning was an open concern in September with the likes of Vertiv, Schneider, etc. dominating at scale. That critical part of the thesis has not been helped by the drop in gross margins. Winning demand at lower margins does not yet support a reasonably competitive moat structure.

Roping In Valuations Now

The primary bone of contention in September was valuations. The EV to forward revenues was ~4.35x, which looked too high for an industrials small cap without clear catalysts pointing to a regime change to a data center-led growth. That part of the thesis has altered significantly. Since my September thesis, PSIX valuation multiples have contracted meaningfully and are now ~2.4x - a derating that has worked out faster than I had anticipated.

Combined with growth and income trends and some hints of actual and consistent data center (power systems) led demand, the valuation reset looks like a compelling opportunity for fresh capital deployment in PSIX now. The gap with Vertiv is also palpable. In September, I had noted the EV to forward revenues for Vertiv at ~5.5x - it is now above ~6x. The gap has widened to account for the 10x revenue scale in Vertiv and a somewhat proven competitive position.

Turning to my DCF analysis, I had found a fair value of closer to $55 accounting for a scenario of revenue growth starting from ~30% growth levels and slowing towards 8% (at a scale of ~$1.3b) in 5 years. Coincidentally, those were the same levels from where PSIX found support and has rebounded from recently. The outlook for 2025 is ~45% growth now, and the past two blockbuster quarters have even shown growth north of 60% to make those assumptions more than achievable.

On the other hand, the FCF assumptions of 12% in Year 1 and improving incrementally every year look challenged now, as production ramp phases could drag free cash flow. And more importantly, gross margins are under pressure.

I reran the DCF with an upgrade on growth assumptions, but pulling down FCF assumptions due to the current gross margin concerns. The current range of fair value estimates have improved from the $55 range indicated in September to within striking distance of where we are now.

Turning Into A Cautious Buy

For those who could accumulate PSIX at $50, the risk-reward seems very well-balanced with a reasonable margin for error. At $65-70, the risk reward is less compelling, but close to a Buy. In September, I had mentioned that any drop below $80 would have me take a look at PSIX. That was not to say that $80 would immediately qualify as a Buy - my DCF fair value anchor would have still weighed in. My $80 mark was more indicative of the prevalent market conditions then, where narratives and momentum counted for more and strong rallies in similar stocks were the norm. In those markets, marginal but sustained overvaluation on growth and potential narratives (particularly a data center angle attached) was allowed.

Since then, PSIX has held its end of the bargain by delivering a valuation reset and confirmed growth trends. Therefore, I would overall rate PSIX a cautious Buy. A Buy because of the fundamental progress and valuations closer to a fair value estimate. Cautious because gross margins are still concerning, and the markets overall have changed. Over the past month or two, several narrative-based stocks and AI-adjacent names have corrected heavily. SMCI has corrected ~40%. Some, like Palantir, have rebounded after correcting by over 25%. Each stock has a different story, but markets are no longer looking like the unidirectional run from April to October. Going ahead, narratives and valuations may continue to be challenged, and while markets may reward narratives in pockets, unhindered valuation expansion across the market breadth looks less probable.

Which is why I am issuing a cautious Buy on PSIX despite the stock ticking most of my checklist today (barring margins). I recommend accumulation in PSIX with a view to lapping up some more (fundamentals allowing) if the stock drops further towards the $50-55 mark. Investors will also need to keep an eye on the full-year results to ensure the margin story does not deteriorate further.
